annotations.text: In the absence of other findings, hemorrhoids are common and not necessarily worrisome. However, the presence of other anorectal abnormalities in this patient raises doubts about whether he really had hemorrhoids.

Conditions that have a similar appearance to hemorrhoids include skin tags (which may be tender if they are associated with Crohn’s disease or chronic fissures), condylomata acuminata, condylomata lata, and anal tuberculosis.
The presence of lateral fissures or of multiple fissures makes underlying systemic disease more likely.
When considering a diagnosis of infection, a timeline from the exposure to the onset of symptoms can be a useful tool in formulating a differential diagnosis.

The description of this patient’s proctitis is not consistent with ulceration, and therefore, we can rule out several common causes of ulcerative proctitis, including herpes simplex virus–associated proctitis, chancroid, and granuloma inguinale caused by the bacteria  *Klebsiella granulomatis*
On the basis of the patient’s sexual history, syphilis is more likely than inflammatory bowel disease.
The onset of secondary syphilis typically occurs 4 to 12 weeks after exposure; although symptoms typically resolve in 3 to 12 weeks, they may last for months.
Several features of this patient’s presentation are suggestive of Crohn’s disease, which is relatively common, with an incidence of approximately 10 cases per 100,000 persons.
If we rule out all these entities from our differential diagnosis, we are left with Crohn’s disease and a number of infectious diseases.
Between January 2013 and January 2014, the number of reported cases of syphilis increased by 15%, and in 2014, a total of 83% of men with primary and secondary syphilis were in the MSM population.
Rash is a nearly universal feature of secondary syphilis, affecting 93% of MSM with secondary syphilis, so its absence should give us pause.
Overall, the features and timeline in this case are consistent with syphilis.
Does this patient have  *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* infection? When considering the diagnosis of tuberculosis, the timeline is usually not helpful because the onset of symptoms can vary
Because this diagnosis would rely on the coexistence of two very rare forms of tuberculosis, it seems unlikely.
In a review of cases in which MSM presented to a surgery clinic for treatment of proctitis, syphilis was a more common cause than Crohn’s disease.
The symptoms in this patient most likely reflect secondary syphilis.

Physical and endoscopic examination may reveal snail-track ulcers that mimic Crohn’s disease, warty lesions that mimic condylomata acuminata, rectal masses that mimic cancer, and colitis that mimics inflammatory bowel disease (as in this case).
This case shows a central issue with the diagnosis of sexually transmitted infections: it can be difficult to identify a causative agent on the basis of history and physical examination alone.
The two most common reasons for missing a treatable cause of anal symptoms are that patients attribute their symptoms to hemorrhoids and that doctors believe them.
